coredata.dc:title: A novel trimodal system on a paper-based microfluidic device for on-site detection of the date rape drug “ketamine”
coredata.dc:creator: Yehia, Ali M.
Farag, Mohamed A.
Tantawy, Mahmoud A.
coredata.dc:description: Paper-based microfluidic device was designed with wax-printing to combine potentiometric, fluorimetric and colorimetric detection zones. This newly developed trimodal paper chip has been used for on-site determination of ketamine hydrochloride (KET) as a date rape drug in beverages. The device employed polyaniline nano-dispersion as conducting polymer in ion sensing paper electrodes designed to fit USB plug connector. Carbon dots-gold nanoparticles and cobalt thiocyanate were used in fluorescence and color detection zones, respectively. Cellular phone’s camera facilitated the on-site fluorimetric and color detection. The implemented trimodal detection system exhibited specificity for KET detection in the presence of several other beverage interferences i.e., biogenic amines. This innovative sensor brings together analytical figures of merit for effective KET detection in single aliquot of spiked beverages. The proposed paper-based chip also fulfils WHO criteria for point-of-care devices posing the proposed trimodal paper device as an active part for rapid, on-site drug diagnostics and to be applied further for other similar drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based device
Potentiometry
Optical sensors
Date rape drugs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescence and color reagents
Design and fabrication of paper device
Interference studies and application of KET detection device
Validation of the KET detection device

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Mohamed Farag acknowledges the funding received from Alexander von Humboldt foundation, Germany and an internal research support grant from the American University in Cairo, Egypt . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photograph of the two wax-printed paper layers (A) ion-sensing PAD for KET where two electrodes are printed using MWCNTs ink on a wax-melted filter paper for further application of PVC indicator and reference membranes; (B) Sampling PAD with three zones corresponding to detection mechanisms; (C) complete device for KET detection where the two layer are aligned.

(A)UV–vis absorption spectra of PANI-SDS in the ES form (green curve) and after dedoping to the EB form (blue curve) using 0.5 M NaOH. Inset: Base dedoping of ES to EB; A− denotes an anion. (B) TEM of PANI-SDS dispersion imaged at 250 k times magnification at 100 keV. (C) Effect of pH on potential stability of KET in beaker-based measurements. (D) Measured potential versus KET standard solutions; miniaturized reference electrode against commercial reference one (green line); miniaturized reference and indicator electrodes in paper based (blue) and beaker based (red) measurements (10−7 – 10−2 mol L−1). Inset; Electrochemical set up of reference and indicator electrodes connected to USB plug and a paper strip with wax-printed boarder placed above the electrodes to hold the sample. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption (dotted curve) and photoluminescence (emission solid curves) spectra of a dilute aqueous solution of C-dots (0.2 mg mL−1) at various excitation wavelengths (350–500 nm); inset TEM of C-dots imaged at 250 k times magnification at 100 keV. (B) Emission spectra @ 350 nm excitation and photographs (inset vials excited @ 365 nm) of C-dots (vial 1, red curve), C-dots/AuNPs (vial 2, grey curve) and C-dots/AuNPs/KET (vial 3, blue curve) dispersed in ultrapure water. (C) UV–vis spectra and photographs (inset) of the AuNPs (tube 4, red curve) and AuNPs/KET (vial 5, purple curve) dispersed in ultrapure water. TEM of AuNPs in absence of KET (D) and in presence of KET (E) imaged at 250 k times magnification at 100 keV. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Photographs of fluorescent spots of C-dots (1), C-dots/AuNPS (2) and C-dots/AuNPs/KET (3) on paper substrate @ 395 nm excitation using UV-LED. (B) Calibration curve of visualized spots after RGB analysis and blank correction with PCR-to-Go for different concentrations of KET. (C) Photographs of color spots of Co(SCN)2/NaOH (blank) and after application of different concentration of KET (10–100 mg mL−1) color changes from light blue to pastel purple; french lilac; old lavender; dark lavender; regalia; and dark slate blue colors. (D) Photographs of color changes after addition of Coca-Cola® (1), Fanta® (2) Barbican® (3) soft drinks and Mineral water (4) with color names cadet grey, dark sea green, cadet blue and moonstone blue respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) KET-sensor connected to USB plug in day light prior to (1) and after (2) the application of KET spiked beverage solution (1 mg mL−1); KET-sensor under UV-LED (395 nm) prior to (3) and after (4) addition of KET spiked beverages (10−3 mol L−1). (B) stability of independent sensors studied over ten-day period.

K E T , I
) for potentiometric and fluorimetric signals alongside with the detected color changes for different interference chemicals.

head.ce:title: A novel trimodal system on a paper-based microfluidic device for on-site detection of the date rape drug “ketamine”

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Cairo, 11562, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Cairo, 11562, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Industries, Badr University in Cairo (BUC), Badr City, 11829, Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Industries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Industries, Badr University in Cairo (BUC), Badr City, 11829, Cairo, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmacognosy Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Cairo, 11562, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmacognosy Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Cairo, 11562, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Sciences & Engineering, The American University in Cairo, New Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Sciences & Engineering, The American University in Cairo, New Cairo, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paper-based microfluidic device was designed with wax-printing to combine potentiometric, fluorimetric and colorimetric detection zones. This newly developed trimodal paper chip has been used for on-site determination of ketamine hydrochloride (KET) as a date rape drug in beverages. The device employed polyaniline nano-dispersion as conducting polymer in ion sensing paper electrodes designed to fit USB plug connector. Carbon dots-gold nanoparticles and cobalt thiocyanate were used in fluorescence and color detection zones, respectively. Cellular phone’s camera facilitated the on-site fluorimetric and color detection. The implemented trimodal detection system exhibited specificity for KET detection in the presence of several other beverage interferences i.e., biogenic amines. This innovative sensor brings together analytical figures of merit for effective KET detection in single aliquot of spiked beverages. The proposed paper-based chip also fulfils WHO criteria for point-of-care devices posing the proposed trimodal paper device as an active part for rapid, on-site drug diagnostics and to be applied further for other similar drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Analgesia and amnesia are characteristic features of dissociative anaesthetics. Impaired sensory function and poor communication of the nervous system create a status of disconnection between the mind and body [1]. Ketamine hydrochloride (KET) is a world-famous dissociative anaesthetic drug that was approved by the FDA in 1970 for induction and maintenance of anaesthesia. It is a non-competitive N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor antagonist [2], though sometimes abused for leisure purposes among the youngsters owing to its ‘dissociative’ state with amnesia [3]. Such colourless, odourless and tasteless ‘club drug’ is frequently spiked in drinks to facilitate sexual assaults. Hallucinating effect of KET is characterized by depersonalization with distort victim’s perception of space and time making them more vulnerable to sexual assaults [4].

Several spectrophotometric [5], liquid [6–10] or gas [11–13] chromatographic, and potentiometric [14] methods have been reported for the determination of KET levels in bulk powder or dosage forms. In spite of its accuracy and sensitivity, these methods fail to be applied on-site and or for rapid detection requiring access to expensive devices. Consequently, point-of-care devices have been developed to overcome this problem with the mounting concern about this date rape drug. An electrochemical microfluidic paper-based device has been developed to employ zeolites nanoflakes/graphene-oxide nanocrystals modified electrode for the voltammetric determination of KET [15]. Another colorimetric KET sensors have been developed on paper [16], silica nanoparticles [17] and poly-dimethylsiloxane [18] substrates. All of these optical sensors are based on Morris color test reaction [19] in which cobalt thiocyanate salt produces purple complex with KET in an alkaline medium.

‘‘Paper’’ has drawn attention as a portable, inexpensive and simple material for point-of-care devices. In terms of detection mechanism, paper-based analytical devices (PAD) employ electrochemical, optical or hybrid sensing systems. Previously, miniaturized ion selective electrodes have been designed on paper strips using carbon nanotubes [20], carbon nanotubes with sputtered gold and polypyrrole [21] or only polypyrrole nanoparticles [22], though with conventional reference electrodes used in measurement. Other attempts for miniaturized indicator and reference paper-based electrodes have been reported using graphene [23], graphite pencil [24] or gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [25] as a conducting material that was insulated in thermoplastic polymers. Rather than thermoplastic, wax imprinting is recently used for the fabrication of all-solid state paper-based potentiometric sensors [26] and for fully integrated liquid contact electrodes [27] where wax is used to define reference, indicator and sample zones on a paper substrate. On the other hand, optical detection is a common sensing mechanism in paper microfluidic devices. With the advancements in using nanomaterials for color and fluorescence sensors [28,29], its implementation as probes in paper-based devices now follows. For example, carbon dots (C-dots) represent one of the accidently discovered fluorescent quantum dots that are characterized by high quantum yield broad excitation and tuneable emission spectra [30]. Meanwhile, AuNPs are efficient fluorescence quencher of C-dots owing to its high extension coefficient and broad absorption range. In that context, the superiority of such donor–acceptor pair quenching mechanism has been employed in some paper-based fluorescent probes [31,32]. Recently, a dual mode paper-device for iron health tests has been developed [33] where Fe3+ ion is detected by fluorescent C-dots, whereas ferritin is detected calorimetrically by Au-NPs coated on none-fluorescent C-dots. Additionally, electrochemical and colorimetric detections of metals have been combined either to screen an interfering ion [34], or to detect several ions simultaneously [35].

In this study, we have developed the first hybrid sensing paper-based device for KET detection. The device combines potentiometric, fluorimetric and colorimetric sensors for sensitive and specific detection of KET. Sampling zones are defined by wax printing on a filter paper positioned above conductive paper-based electrodes. The electrodes are printed on paper substrate with drop casted membranes and designed for USB plug connection. Single aliquot of sample is applied first on the potentiometric detection zone after which it spreads to the fluorimetric and colorimetric detection zones where reagents are dried. C-dots–AuNPs pair are applied in the fluorimetric zone, whereas cobalt thiocyanate and NaOH are applied in the color zone. This developed device attained good accuracy and sensitivity depending on detection methods. The combination of the three detection modes on one substrate increase device selectivity towards KET detection in the presence of other interferences. This paper-based chip has been mapped on WHO criteria for point-of-care devices and was found quite effective for the determination of KET in spiked beverages.

Pure ketamine hydrochloride powder (99.69%) was supplied by Supriya lifescience Ltd., India. Sodium tetrakis-[3,5-bis(trifluoro-methyl)phenyl] borate (TFPB) and hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), potasium tetrakis(p-chlorophenyl)borate (TpClPB), tetradodecyl ammonium bromide (TDAB), calix [6]arene (CX6), 2-nitrophenyl octyl ether (NPOE), dibutyl sebacate (DBS), sodium dodecyl suphate (SDS), aniline, ammonium persulphate, urea, trisodium citrate, cobalt chloride and ammonium thiocyanate, caffeine (99%), tryptamine (98%), phenylethylamine (≥99%), tyramine (≥98%), histamine (≥97%) and ethanol (≥99.8%)) were all purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Neurobion ampoules were purchased from local market (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany) containing 100 mg vitamin B1 (thiamine hydrochloride), 100 mg vitamin B6 (pyridoxine hydrochloride), 1000 μg vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) in 3 ml of aqueous solution. Red Bull® energy drink (Red Bull GmbH, Austria). Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) was obtained from Fluka Chemie GmbH (St. Louis, MO, USA). Tetrahydrofuran (THF) was obtained from BDH (Poole, England). Ultrapure water (
≤
18.3 MΩ cm) was prepared using New human power water purification system (Human corporation, Seoul, Korea). Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared by mixing equal volumes of acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid (0.04 mol L−1 each) adjusted to the required pH (2−11) by 0.2 mol L−1 NaOH standard solution.

Xerox® ColorQube® 8700 Color Multifunction Printer, Xerox, Norwalk, CT, USA; SHIMADZU dual beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan), model UV-1650; Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) JOEL USA, Inc., model JEM-1230 with carbon-coated copper grid; Reference electrode (Thermo Scientific Orion double junction Ag/AgCl reference electrode No. 900200, MA, US) with 10% KNO3 as bridge electrolyte and 3 mol L−1 KCl saturated with AgCl as reference electrolyte. Potential measurements were performed using Jenway Digital Ion Analyzer (Model No. 3330; Essex, United Kingdom) in magnetically stirred solution at ambient temperature (25±2 °C). pH Adjustments were carried out using Jenway pH glass electrode (No. 6.0133.100, Metrohm AG, CH-9010 Herisau, Switzerland). Agilent Technologies Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (G9800A Fluorescence). An iPhone6-based camera reader and ultraviolet-LED torch with 395 nm light (Winzwon) were used as a portable source of excitation wavelength for fluorimetric detection. PCR-to-Go (Aashish Priye© Ugaz Group, Ver 2) and Color-Name-AR (Vlad Polyanskiy©) are free Appstore applications used for fluorescence and color detection.

A solution of MWCNTs (3 mg mL−1) was prepared in an aqueous solution of SDS (10%w/v) and used as carbon ink following the protocol of Cui et al. [36]. Polyaniline nanoparticles (PANI) were prepared by chemical polymerization as previously reported by Moulton et al. [37]. Briefly, 100 mL of ammonium persulphate (1.3 mol L−1) was added dropwise to a 100 mL of mechanically stirred aniline/SDS (1.3 mol L−1 each) mixture. The mixture was left at constant temperature of 20 °C and the polymerization proceeded for further 2.5 h where the solution gradually turns from milky white to a dark green dispersion. PANI was purified by dialysis followed by centrifugation. The final nanoparticles were dispersed in chloroform (10%w/v) and protected from light. PANI were characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy and TEM measurements. Ion selective membrane was prepared by mixing TFPB (5 mmol kg−1) and CX6 (10 mmol kg−1) with 66 wt% NPOE and 33 wt% PVC. Reference membrane preparation was prepared by mixing 2 × 10−2 mmol/kg of each of TpClPB and TDAB with 68 wt% of DBS and 30 wt% PVC. A total of 600 mg of each membrane solutes were dissolved in 4 mL THF and sonicated for 10 min to form a solution of 15 wt%. Amounts of ground KCl (0.16 g) and AgCl (0.06 g with spontaneously formed traces of metallic silver) was sonicated with 1 mL aliquot of the prepared reference membrane solution for 15 min prior to its application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescence and color reagents
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave-assisted bottom-up synthesis protocol of water-soluble C-dots described in Ref. [38] was adopted. Briefly, 3.0 g of citric acid and urea were dissolved in 10 mL water then heated using a 750 W microwave oven for 5 min. The formed dark-brown foamy clusters were placed in a vacuum oven for 1 h at 60 °C. Aqueous solution of C-dots (1 mg mL−1) was prepared and centrifugated at 3000 rpm for 20 min to get rid of any residual particles. Citrate-capped AuNPs were prepared according to Frens’ method [39] where 50 mL of HAuCl4 (0.01 wt%) was heated to boiling, then 0.5 mL aqueous solution of trisodium citrate (1 wt%) was added. The color of dispersion changed from dark blue of nucleation to a stable wine red suspension of AuNPs in ca.15 min. The prepared nanoparticles were characterized using UV–vis spectroscopy and TEM images. Cobalt thiocyanate was prepared by mixing cobalt chloride (2.90 mg mL−1) with boiled ammonium thiocyanate (3.04 g mL−1) aqueous solution as previously reported [40]. The blue residual complex was collected post evaporation and drying at about 90 °C. Aqueous solutions of cobalt thiocyanate (0.02 mg mL−1) and sodium hydroxide (4 mg mL−1) were prepared in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of paper device
ce:section.ce:para: The design of paper device was patterned using Microsoft Office PowerPoint 2010 and printed using a wax printer on filters RS (M. Jean-Pierre Durieux, France), a round filter Ø. 400 mm after cutting to A4 size. The device consists of solid state electrodes and a sample strip, Fig. (1) . Wax was melted in an electrical oven at 100 °C for about 5 min creating hydrophobic barriers. The template for ion-sensing PAD were imprinted such that the electrode leads were spaced 8 mm apart to fit into the USB female port connector. MWCNT ink was applied to the defined electrode zones using glass micro-pipette, Fig. 1A. About six cycles were repeated with oven drying (60 °C) in between till the dried electrode resistance reached 400 ± 50 Ω. An adhesive tape with 2 punched holes (6 mm diameter each) was placed on the electrode surface followed by application of 10 μL PANI dispersion once and subsequent membrane solution. The indicator and reference membrane solutions (10 μL each) were applied three times on their respective zones and allowed for 10 min air drying between applications. The electrodes were conditioned by applying 10−3 M KET and 3 M KCl solution on the indicator and reference electrodes, respectively, and left for 4 h in a small closed container. The second strip is sampling PAD (23 × 20 mm), Fig. 1B, combining three detection zones designated as “K” letter denoting the street name of KET. Reagents were applied and dried at the end of “K” arms. C-dots solution was applied in fluorescence detection zone followed by AuNPs. CO(SCN)2 solution was applied in color detection zone followed by NaOH solution. Yellow contrasting boarder were printed around the colorimetric detection zone, in order to distinguish the color reaction zone and to further enhance color change detection. Finally, the two strips were put together such that the potentiometric zone of sample strip was positioned on the ion-sensing EPAD (Fig. 1C). The device was covered with adhesive tape from the back side. In such paper design, applied sample on the potentiometric sampling zone should spontaneously split in fluorimetric and colorimetric detection zones.

The printed electrodes were first examined by beaker-based measurements. The reference electrode in conjunction with conventional reference one was used to measure the potential of KET standard solutions to evaluate its potential stability. Both miniaturized indicator and reference electrodes were connected to a potentiometer via USB plug and used for measuring the potential of stirred KET standard solutions. Effect of pH was examined at two levels (10−3 and 10−4 M) of KET solutions to determine the working pH range. Beaker-based measurements were also used to evaluate the static response time of the electrode. Finally, the calibration was constructed by paper-based measurements. A disposable filter paper with wax printed boarder was used to hold the KET standard solutions (10 μL aliquots) in a defined zone. The electrode surface was wiped by filter paper between measurements. Stability of electrodes was monitored for 2 months.

Wax printed circular reaction reservoirs were used to optimize the concentration of optical reagents. These discs (6 mm diameter each) were arranged on black background for optimization of the fluorimentric detection zone. Discs were loaded with 10 μL aliquots at different concentrations of C-dots solution (1 mg mL−1) followed by 20 μL of AuNPs solution. The zones were visualized under UV-LED torch (@395 nm) to monitor the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the quenching efficiency of AuNPs. Ten μL aliquots of KET standard solutions (2 × 10−4 – 1 × 10−3 mol L−1) were transferred to circular zones after air dying of the reagents. The restored fluorescence of C-dots is visualized by a UV-LED torch after 1 min incubation period then photos were captured for the zones and analysed using PCR-to-Go application. Calibration curves was constructed relating the maximum values of red, green and blue intensities to the corresponding concentrations of KET. Same discs were used for optimizing the colorimetric detection zone, albeit with yellow background. Zones were loaded with 10 μL of each of cobalt thiocyanate (0.02 mg mL−1) and sodium hydroxide (4 mg mL−1) solution. After drying, 10 μL aliquot of KET standard solutions (10–100 mg mL−1; equivalent to 3.63 × 10−2 – 3.63 × 10−1 mol L−1) were transferred to the color zones. The blue color was instantaneously changed to lavender or purplish blue color and detected by Color-Name-AR application.

In the context of miniaturization, sensors are made ready and feasible for less experienced end-users. One of the important applications of user-friendly sensors is KET as a date rape drug. Herein we devised a single paper chip sensor with three detection mechanisms to improve the selectivity of KET detection at a wide range of beverages. KET is chemically designated as (2-(2-chlorophenyl)- 2-(methylamino)-cyclohexanone). This arylcycloalkylamine possesses a secondary amino functionality with pKã7, acting as a Lewis base at pH value below 7 and to thus acquire a positive charge. This conjugate acid form of the drug is responsible for the potentiometric and fluorimetric sensing mechanism applied in this study. For instance, the negatively charged membrane is likely to sense the positively charged drug ion using potentiometric sensor. The positive amino functionality in KET may also further destabilize the citrate-capped AuNPs for subsequent disruption of inner filter effect mechanism in quenched C-dots-AuNPs pair for fluorimetric sensor. On the other hand, a recognized color reaction of KET with Co(SCN)2 in alkaline medium is utilized for optical KET detection. Presumably, the relatively hydrophobic substituent on the protonated amino functionality of KET (log p = 2.9) stabilizes the coordination sphere of 2:1 KET–Co(SCN)4 ion-associate complex [40]. In order to combine the three sensing mechanisms, a paper strip was designed using wax printing to provide an efficient control of sample flow in cellulose fibres heading to the three different detection zones. Optimization of each sensor is described in the next subsections followed by the assembly of such innovative trimodal design on a single chip.

Conducting polymers are usually deposited on an electron-conducting substrates to facilitate ion-to-electron transduction via its doping effect. Application of conducting polymer improves the integrity of paper-based electrodes by elimination of water-containing compartment therefore enabling the application in nonaqueous solvents [41]. Among the conducting polymers examined in these systems was polypyrrole [21], polypyrrole nanoparticles [22], poly-(3-octylthiophene) [24] or poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)/poly (styrenesulfonate) mixture [25,26]. Polyaniline nanoparticle is one of the conducting polymers that has been proved for insignificant potential drift and surpassing inter-electrode potential reproducibility in solid contact macro-systems [42]. Protonation and deprotonation of PANI as a conducting polymer was characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2 A). The absorption spectrum of PANI-SDS dispersion below pH 2 exhibited localized polaron bands at 785 and 420 nm due to the formation of conducting emeraldine salt [43]. Also Π–Π* band at approximately 350 nm was presumably overlapped with polaron band at 420 nm to form distorted single peak indicating a high level of doping [44]. This Π–Π* band is blue shifted to 320 nm upon base treatment. Fig. 2A shows the spectrum of formed emeraldine base possessing a peak at 560 nm corresponding to the excitation band of the deprotonated form [45]. The particle size was investigated using TEM (Fig. 2B) with an average diameter of 10 ± 4 nm (Fig. 1S). The proposed miniaturized paper-based electrodes utilized PANI as a transducer layer between MWCNTs conducting base and PVC membranes. KET sensing membrane was incorporated with TFPB as a cation exchanger and CX6 as an ionophore. This liphophilic borate derivative is a suitable exchanger for the basic amino functionality in KET, whereas the ionophore functions to improve the membrane response towards the target ion through intermolecular forces. Preliminary studies revealed ca. half order of magnitude increase in detection limit for ionophore free membrane and to confirm the favoured binding forces between KET and crown ether [46]. The proposed miniaturized electrode showed stable potential from pH 3 to 6 (Fig. 2C), likely attributed to ionization of KET below pH 6 while decreased response is observed above pH 7 due to the formation of KET conjugate base. On the other hand, reference PVC membrane is commonly prepared using tetrabutylammonium-tetrabutylborate in paper-based systems 24−26 . Rather than this single-salt, a double-salt approach has been applied for stable reference macro-electrodes using borate and ammonium salts as lipophilic cation and anion exchangers, respectively [47]. In a comparative study, Mattinen et al. [48] indicated that this double salt approach has a faster response which suits our proposed disposable electrochemical cell. The proposed miniaturized reference electrode comprises TpClPB and TDAB salts is examined in conjunction with commercial silver/silver chloride reference electrode using beaker-based measurements (Fig. 2D). A stable potential for different KET solutions at pH 6 was recorded that proved the stability of the proposed miniaturized reference electrode. Potential profile was recorded using the miniaturized indicator and reference paper-based electrodes (Fig. 2D). The electrodes are connected to USB plug and immersed in a stirred KET standard solution at pH 6 in beaker-based measurements. The response time was found to be ca. 20 sec. Ten microliters of KET standard solutions (10−7 – 10−2 mol L−1) were applied on disposable filter paper for paper-based measurements. The measurement set-up used in this work is depicted in Fig. 2D (inset) where wax printed borders confine the sample solution. This uniform sample distribution across the thin paper strip to vertically aliened electrodes requires less equilibration time and minimizes the probability of sample evaporation [49]. Nernstian response was registered in the range of (10−5 – 10−2 mol L−1) and with the linear fitting equation found to be E = 59.2*C + 432.2 (R2 = 0.9993), (Fig. 2D). The figure also shows the higher detection limit of paper-based measurement compared to beaker-based one (ca. six-tenths order of magnitude) that may be attributed to convection free transport employed in paper devices.

Fluorescence sensor for KET analysis is designed on paper substrate using a high quantum yield C-dots. The absorption spectrum of C-dots aqueous solution (0.2 mg mL−1) shows typical absorptions peaks of aromatic Π -system at 270 nm, 340 nm, and 405 nm indicating the extended conjugation of the C-dots structure (Fig. 3 A). The same figure shows the excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence property of C-dots. Upon increasing excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 500 nm, red shift in the emission maxima were observed. The intense photoluminescence was observed at 453 nm (blue luminescence) and 519 nm (green luminescence) upon excitation at 350 nm and 400 nm, respectively. TEM image of C-dots showed spherical and well dispersed nanoparticles with an average diameter of 8 nm (Fig. 3A, inset). The aqueous solution of C-dots emits strong bluish luminescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3B, inset 1). This luminescence is efficiently quenched by the addition of AuNPs (1:2 C-dots/AuNPs by volume), (Fig. 3B, inset 2). The visible spectrum of AuNPs dispersion showed a maximum absorbance at 527 nm with high extension coefficient (Fig. 3C, inset 4), that was overlapped with C-dots emission. Since there is electrostatic repulsive force between carboxylic groups of C-dots and the negatively charged AuNPs, the observed quenching mechanism is likely attributed to the inner filter effect. This mechanism is evidenced by the similarity between absorption spectrum of C-dots/AuNPs and the individual spectra (Fig. 2S) indicating no complexation between the pair and that the inner filter effect is indeed the dominant quenching mechanism. AuNPs are aggregated by the addition of KET solution (10−3 mol L−1) and its absorption maxima is shifted to about 660 nm forming a blue color (Fig. 3C, inset 5). Fig. 3D shows the spherically dispersed AuNPs (13 nm average diameter) and their aggregation after addition of KET as illustrated in Fig. 3E. The reason for such an aggregation is attributed to amino group of KET that may strongly bind to the surface of AuNPs by the ligand exchange with citrate ions thus decreasing the electrostatic repulsion between individual AuNPs [50]. Upon addition of KET solution to C-dots-AuNPs pairs, aggregation of AuNPs occur and result in a disruption of inner filter effect (Fig. 3B, inset 3). This mechanism provides an optical signal of restored C-dots fluorescence for KET detection, with fluorescence intensities being unaffected at pH range 3–7. At strong acidic or basic solutions, the fluorescence is either weak or unreliable due to the instability of AuNPs. The prepared C-dots are recognized for their stability on cellulose paper compared to other substrates which are favoured to the proposed paper-based device [38]. Ten μL aliquot of C-dots solution (1 mg mL−1) produces a detectable fluorescence on paper substrate. Fig. 4 A show C-dots-coated paper that retains the greenish luminescence upon excitation at 395 nm using UV-LED torch. Such fluorescence is then immediately quenched after the application of 20 μL of AuNPs dispersed solution, likely due to the close physical contact between donor and acceptor particles on paper substrate for inner filter effect mechanism. Upon applying KET solution to the quenched spots, C-dots start to emit and reach a stable fluorescence after 1 min incubation period. The restored fluorescence intensity of C-dots is related to KET concentrations for quantitative analysis (Fig. 4B). A good linear correlation over a wide range of concentrations (2 × 10−4 – 1 × 10−3 mol L−1) is recognized for the red intensity after blank correction while green and blue intensities show limited linearity and outlier data, respectively. The linear fitting equation of red intensity is F–F0 = 123991*C – 13.75 (R2 = 0.9975) obtained for KET buffered solution (pH 7.0) and used for further analysis. Color sensor certainly satisfies the end user need for a facile detection. Although a blue colour appears after the addition of KET to AuNPs dispersion (inset 5 in Fig. 3C), it is hardly distinguished on paper substrate. For such reason, the device is integrated with modified Morris reaction for feasible color detection mechanism, with Color-Name-AR used to find an objective color assessment. Fig. 4C shows a gradual color change from blue (blank) to pastel purple (lower KET concentrations) or dark slate blue (higher KET concentrations). It is obvious that the working range of color sensor is much higher that the proposed potentiometric and fluorimetric ranges, posing color sensor to be used only for qualitative analysis as in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies and application of KET detection device
ce:section.ce:para:
Beverages can be categorized into non-alcoholic and alcoholic drinks according to food and agriculture organization. Alcohol free drinks i.e., carbonated soft drinks contain carbonic acid, phosphoric acid, citric acid, and caffeine while exotic fruit juices are noncarbonated and to encompass usually ascorbic acid, polyphenols, and carotenoids. In contrast, energy drinks contain caffeine, glucuronolactone, amino acids, herb extracts and B-vitamins. Alcoholic beverages contain ethyl alcohol with considerable amounts of biogenic amines produced during fermentation [51] which might interfere with KET detection. Selectivity coefficients (
K
K E T , I
) were calculated for 1 × 10−3 mol L−1 of possible organic interferences from chemicals naturally found in beverages using same potentiometric and fluorometric sensor. Table (1) shows low coefficients for caffeine and B-vitamins, whereas certain lipophilic biogenic amines such as tryptamine and phenylethylamine (log p ≈ 1.4) interfere with the potentiometric determination of KET. In addition, histamine exhibits a measurable fluorescence (
K
K E T , I
= 2.10 × 10−1). Even if trytptamine, phenylethylamine and histamine interfere with potentiometric and fluorimetric KET detection, the proposed sensor is still applicable as the average level of these biogenic amines [52] is 5 × 10−5 mol L−1 and their contribution is thus considered insignificant. For instance, the maximum calculated error in the fluorimetric determination of KET is about 2% in the presence of maximum level of histamine (1.8 × 10−5 mol L−1). In contrast, colorimetric sensor is evident to be highly selective to KET where color is hardly changed for most of the interferences (10 mg mL−1). Only a clearly distinguishable camel color is formed in case of histamine that recedes histamine possibility of fluorescence interreference, Table (1). Considering that most of soft drinks are acidic (pH ∼ 3) to neutral (pH∼7), this range is appropriate for electrochemical and fluorescent detection, whereas 0.1 M NaOH sufficiently renders the medium alkaline for color detection. Colored beverages may also interfere with the detection mechanism. Consequently, a range of drinks were applied on color zones (Fig. 4D) and compared to blank Co(SCN)2/NaOH (spot (0) in Fig. 4C). Color change was hardy detected in each of the studied blank colored samples or in mineral water. Possible interfering mineral salts were tested as well for their potentiometric and fluorimetric signals (Table S1). The calculated selectivity coefficients were too low to be considered. A positive control sample was prepared for real application in commercial beverage analysis. Red Bull® energy drink was spiked with KET (ca.10−3 mol L−1; pH ≈ 3.9) then directly applied to potentiometric sample zone in KET-sensor. The applied single aliquot spontaneously seeps into fluorescence and color zones (Fig. 5 A). Average recoveries of independent measurements using six devices were 101.28 ± 2.75 and 100.83 ± 4.84 for potentiometric and fluorimetric sensors, respectively. Fig. 5A also shows that color zone is changed from light blue to pastel purple after application of KET spiked Red Bull® energy drink (ca. 3.6 × 10−2 mol L−1; pH ≈ 3.4). Color change could be easily noticed without interference from the yellow colored beverage or the blue coloration of CO(SCN)2 residue in the channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the KET detection device

ce:section.ce:para: Utilization of nanoparticles in the proposed wax-printed KET paper-based device combines the merits of three detection systems and posing it as a potential point-of-care device for KET on site detection. The device attained accuracy, sensitivity, selectivity and feasibility of electrochemical and fluorescence determination. In addition, colorimetric sensor bears the advantage of being simple and selective for the qualitative detection of KET. The combination of the three detection mechanisms improves the specificity of the proposed sensor for KET detection over a specified concentrations range. In conclusion, the developed point-of-care device for KET detection fulfils WHO criteria and could efficiently alleviate the hazard consequences of this date rape drug. This hybrid paper device provides a platform for multiplex detection of date rape drugs suggestive for a wide range of future further applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Mohamed Farag acknowledges the funding received from Alexander von Humboldt foundation, Germany and an internal research support grant from the American University in Cairo, Egypt.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.002.
